title: Remotes & Pull Requests: Collaborating on Purpose
learningObjectives: Connect a local repository to a remote and push/pull changes correctly
Explain the difference between fetch and pull
Open a pull request that clearly describes what changed and why, even solo
Review a pull request's diff critically before merging it, rather than merging on trust alone
whyItMatters: Every commit made so far has lived only on one machine. The AI Energy OS eventually needs a shared, remote source of truth — and the pull-request workflow (propose a change, review it, then merge) is how real engineering teams keep main trustworthy even as multiple people, or future collaborators, or just future-you reviewing past-you's work, contribute.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's move from 'a local folder of code' to 'a real, remotely hosted, reviewable project' — the point where it becomes something a collaborator, researcher, or future you could actually pick up.

problem.summary: Everything built so far exists on only one machine.
problem.content: Everything you've built lives only on this one machine. If the disk fails, or you want to work from a second machine, or eventually invite a collaborator, none of that is possible yet.

motivation.summary: This is the step that makes the work durable, shareable, and reviewable.
motivation.content: This is the step that turns your local experiment into something durable and shareable. It's also how you'll practice a review discipline — catching your own mistakes before they land on main — that pays off the moment more than one person, or more than one future version of you, touches the code.

theory.summary: Remotes host a copy elsewhere; fetch reviews before integrating, pull integrates immediately.
theory.content: A remote is a reference to a copy of the repository hosted elsewhere (e.g. on GitHub); `git remote add origin <url>` registers it. `git push origin main` sends your local commits to the remote; `git fetch` downloads the remote's latest history without changing your local branches, while `git pull` fetches and immediately merges into your current branch — the distinction matters because fetch lets you review incoming changes before integrating them. A pull request proposes merging one branch into another on the remote host, with a description of what changed and why, and a place for review comments before it's merged.

visualExplanation.summary: Push and pull flow between local and remote; fetch updates a tracking reference without touching local branches.
visualExplanation.content: A local repository and a remote repository are shown as two separate boxes, with push and pull arrows flowing between them, and fetch shown as a one-way arrow that updates a separate 'remote-tracking' reference without touching the local branch.

interactiveDemo.summary: Push a branch, open a mock pull request, review it, then merge.
interactiveDemo.content: Simulate pushing a branch to a remote, open a mock pull request with a description field, add a review comment on a specific line of the diff, then merge it — watching the remote's main branch update only after the merge step.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: register a remote, push, branch, and open a pull request.
guidedCoding.content:
```bash
git remote add origin https://github.com/you/digital-twin.git
git push -u origin main

git switch -c improve-battery-thresholds
# make changes, commit
git push -u origin improve-battery-thresholds
# open a pull request from improve-battery-thresholds into main
```

independentCoding.summary: Push a real branch and write a pull-request description that flags what a reviewer should check.
independentCoding.content: Push a real branch of your own (or a simulated one in the sandbox) to a remote, write a pull-request description that clearly states what changed and why, and list at least two things you'd want a reviewer to specifically check.

debugging.summary: Diagnose an unexpected pull conflict caused by skipping review-before-merge.
debugging.content: A learner ran `git pull` and got a merge conflict they weren't expecting because they hadn't fetched/reviewed first. Walk through using `git fetch` plus `git log` to review incoming changes before merging, as the safer alternative.

reflection.summary: Reviewing before integrating is safer than always pulling directly.
reflection.content: Why might reviewing incoming changes with `git fetch` before merging be safer than always running `git pull` directly, especially on a shared branch?

teachBack.summary: Explain why a pull request exists even on a solo project.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone why a pull request exists as a separate step from just pushing directly to main, even on a solo project.

miniProject.summary: Push, open, review, and merge a real pull request end to end.
miniProject.content: Push your `digital_twin/` repository to a real or simulated remote, open a pull request for your most recent branch, write a clear description, and merge it only after reviewing your own diff as if you were a reviewer seeing it for the first time.

portfolioEvidence.summary: The Digital Twin is now a project with real remote history and review discipline.
portfolioEvidence.content: This is the point where the Digital Twin becomes a project with a real remote history and a review discipline, not just local commits only you have ever seen.

labs.title: Pull Request Workflow
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise simulating a remote host: push a branch, author a pull-request description, add review comments, and merge only after addressing them.

simulations.title: Remote Push/Pull Visualizer
simulations.description: Shows local and remote repositories as separate boxes with push/fetch/pull arrows, distinguishing fetch's tracking-reference update from pull's immediate merge.

flashcards.front: What's the key difference between git fetch and git pull?
flashcards.back: git fetch downloads the remote's latest history without touching your local branches, letting you review it first. git pull fetches and immediately merges into your current branch.

flashcards.front: Why open a pull request instead of pushing directly to main, even when working solo?
flashcards.back: It creates a deliberate review checkpoint — a place to re-read your own diff, document why the change was made, and catch mistakes before they land on the trusted branch, which is a habit worth having before it's ever required by a teammate.

rubric: Pull request description clearly states what changed and why, not just restating the diff
Learner identifies specific, concrete things a reviewer should check rather than a generic 'looks good' checklist
Correctly distinguishes when to use fetch-then-review versus pull directly
